lines.text: The adrenaline hasn't stopped. You can feel it in your hands — still humming, not quite belonging to you yet.
You think it went well. You don't know what that means here. But something in the room changed when you were in it, and you felt it.
Then you realize — there's someone still in the back of the room.
Not a producer. Not a director. Just watching.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: You didn't perform it.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: That instinct — raw, unpolished, but real. Most people lose it within a year of trying. It gets trained out of them, or they trade it for technique.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: You still have it. I don't know yet if you know that.
You recognize her immediately. Amy Crawford. The name carries weight — the kind that gets said quietly, like a warning or a recommendation.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Hm.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Most people would have said 'thank you, I worked really hard.' You just said thank you.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Mild surprise. Keep that too.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (Something shifts in her expression. Not displeasure — closer to the recalibration of someone who underestimated you.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Good. Confidence without performance. That's rarer than the instinct.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (She raises an eyebrow.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Filed.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: One thing you did wrong.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: The third beat, when you crossed downstage — your weight transferred half a count early. It's not visible at audition distance, but the camera would catch it. On tape, it reads as insecurity.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: It's a physical habit. You'll repeat it under pressure unless you isolate it now.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (She watches you. Waiting.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (A slight nod. Not approval exactly — more like something settling into place.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Good answer.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (She pauses. Reconsidering you.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: I would have let the weight settle a beat before the cross. Ground it first. The move means more when the body knows it's coming.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: The camera reads intention before action. Most actors get that backwards.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (A pause. Something careful behind her eyes.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: I'll let you figure that out.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: (Small smile. She doesn't argue.)

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Maybe it did.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: I'm Amy Crawford. You've probably heard things.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Some of them are true. The rest depend on who's telling the story.

lines.speaker: Amy Crawford
lines.text: Take the card. Call it if the work gets serious.
You watch her go. The way she moved through the exit — like she'd decided to leave before she decided to stay.
You're not sure if it was a warning or an invitation. You're not sure she knows the difference.
A matte black card. No title. Just a number.
